components.title: Django
components.description: Django is the Python web framework the organisation's web application(s) are built on. It provides the underlying authentication, session management, permissions, and administrative interface — the deployed application's security controls are implemented by configuring and extending these built-in framework capabilities rather than building them from scratch.
components.purpose: Provide the authentication, access control, session management, and administrative framework underlying the organisation's web application(s).

implemented-requirements.description: Django's login throttling (django-axes or equivalent) locks an account after five consecutive failed login attempts within a defined time window, requiring administrator action to unlock before the lockout period expires.

Django's session framework is configured with SESSION_COOKIE_AGE and SESSION_SAVE_EVERY_REQUEST such that a session is terminated after a maximum of 15 minutes of user inactivity or 12 hours of overall session time, whichever comes first, and terminating a session requires the user to re-authenticate with all configured authentication factors.

Access to the Django admin site is restricted to accounts with the is_staff/is_superuser flag, and those accounts are required to complete multi-factor authentication (via django-otp or an equivalent SSO integration) before the admin interface is reachable.

Django's permissions framework grants each user only the model-level and object-level permissions their role requires; the admin site — the only interface with elevated management capability — is unreachable to accounts without the is_staff flag.

Every Django account is tied to a single, individually-attributable user record — shared or generic accounts are not created, so every action attributable to a Django user (content changes, admin actions) can be traced to one accountable individual.

Django's permissions framework (django.contrib.auth's model- and object-level permissions, combined with view-level decorators/mixins) enforces logical access to application data and functionality — a request is only served if the authenticated user holds the specific permission the view requires.

Django is configured with a login-throttling mechanism (django-axes or equivalent) that locks an account after a defined number of invalid login attempts within a defined time period; the threshold and lockout duration are configurable to conform to organisational policy.

Django's session framework terminates a session after SESSION_COOKIE_AGE elapses or after a period of inactivity (enforced via SESSION_SAVE_EVERY_REQUEST), and sessions can also be terminated on demand — e.g. on password change, via Django's session invalidation hooks.

Django user authentication is performed via django.contrib.auth's username/password backend, or delegated to an organisational Single Sign-On provider via a configured SSO authentication backend — no application-specific authentication mechanism is implemented outside these supported backends.

Django's login form obscures password input as it is typed. On authentication failure, Django returns the same generic error message regardless of whether the username or the password was incorrect, so a failed attempt does not reveal which field was wrong or whether the account exists.

Django is kept on a supported release and updated in line with the Django Security Team's published security releases; the deployed version and its dependencies are tracked so a security advisory can be matched against what's actually running and patched promptly.
